title: First Parish Church in Plymouth

infobox.value: [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places")

is a historic [Unitarian Universalist](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalist "Unitarian Universalist") church at the base of [Burial Hill](/wiki/Burial_Hill "Burial Hill") on the [town square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square") off [Leyden Street](/wiki/Leyden_Street "Leyden Street") in [Plymouth, Massachusetts](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"). The congregation was founded in 1620 by the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") in Plymouth. The current building was constructed in 1899.
Paul Erasmus Lauer, *Church and state in New England* (Johns Hopkins Press, 1892) pp. 105–107 [[1]](https://books.google.com/books?id=2J8TAAAAYAAJ) (accessed September 20, 2009)

**First Parish Church in Plymouth** is a historic [Unitarian Universalist](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalist "Unitarian Universalist") church at the base of [Burial Hill](/wiki/Burial_Hill "Burial Hill") on the [town square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square") off [Leyden Street](/wiki/Leyden_Street "Leyden Street") in [Plymouth, Massachusetts](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"). The congregation was founded in 1620 by the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") in Plymouth. The current building was constructed in 1899.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=First_Parish_Church_in_Plymouth&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Congregation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=First_Parish_Church_in_Plymouth&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Congregation")]

Main article: [Scrooby Congregation](/wiki/Scrooby_Congregation "Scrooby Congregation")

The congregation was formed in the English village of [Scrooby](/wiki/Scrooby "Scrooby"), Nottinghamshire, around 1607 by the [Pilgrim Fathers](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)"), a group of exiled [dissenting](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") in the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"). After they emigrated to America in 1620, the congregation built a chapel in Plymouth which became a parish church of Massachusetts' [state church](/wiki/State_church "State church"), the [Congregational Church](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States"). Eventually, a schism developed in 1801, when much of the congregation adopted [Unitarianism](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") along with many of the other state churches in Massachusetts; the Congregationalist dissenters broke away to form the Church of the Pilgrimage. All state churches were disaffiliated with the government by 1834.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
The congregation is currently affiliated with the [Unitarian Universalist Association](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalist_Association "Unitarian Universalist Association") and has 64 members as of 2016.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=First_Parish_Church_in_Plymouth&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Buildings")]

Originally, the congregation held [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") services on the [Mayflower](/wiki/Mayflower "Mayflower") and then at a fort on [Burial Hill](/wiki/Burial_Hill "Burial Hill") from 1621 until 1648. The fort was also used for other colony events including meetings of the [Plymouth General Court](/wiki/Plymouth_General_Court "Plymouth General Court"). In 1648 the first of four church buildings on the town square was constructed. Later churches were built in 1684, 1744, and 1831. [Hartwell, Richardson & Driver](/wiki/Hartwell_and_Richardson "Hartwell and Richardson") designed the current [Romanesque-style](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") building, completed 1899, which replaced the 1831 [wooden Gothic](/wiki/Carpenter_Gothic "Carpenter Gothic") structure.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The 1899 building was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2014. It has Tiffany [stained glass windows](/wiki/Stained_glass_window "Stained glass window") illustrating the Pilgrim story. The sanctuary features carved quarter-sawn oak and is one of the finest examples of [hammer beam construction](/wiki/Hammerbeam_roof "Hammerbeam roof") in the United States.
